title: 7 Things You Should Know About Stocks
subtitle: What a share of stock is and how stock investing works
hero_takeaways: A stock is a share of ownership in a company
Prices go up and down based on supply and demand
Long-term investing can help smooth out short-term swings

content_blocks.text: When you buy a stock, you own a small piece of that company. If the company does well, your share can become more valuable. If it doesn't, the value can go down.

items: Stocks are also called shares or equity.
Companies sell stock to raise money to grow.
You can buy and sell stocks through a brokerage account.

content_blocks.text: Prices move based on how many people want to buy vs. sell, and on news about the company or the economy.

content_blocks.title: Example: How a stock price might move over time
content_blocks.description: Hypothetical line chart showing ups and downs over 12 months.

content_blocks.title: Keep it simple
content_blocks.text: You don't need to time the market. Many people invest regularly over time (dollar-cost averaging) to smooth out volatility.

example_blocks.text: Alphabet Inc. (ticker: GOOGL) is a company that offers search, cloud, and other tech services. When you buy one share of Alphabet stock, you own a tiny fraction of the company.
This is for illustration only. We are not recommending any specific stock.

video_blocks.text: Animation: A company building with many small tiles. One tile lights up — that's your share.
Animation: A simple order flow from you → brokerage → market.
Animation: A line chart with up/down movement and a "time in market" message.
Animation: Multiple tiles from different companies — don't put all eggs in one basket.

questions.prompt: What do you own when you buy a stock?
choices: A small piece (share) of the company
questions.explanation: A stock is a share of ownership in a company. If the company grows, the value of your share can grow too — but it can also go down.

choices: Based on supply and demand and news
Only when the company pays dividends
questions.explanation: Prices move based on how many people want to buy vs. sell and on news about the company or the economy.

choices: Investing only when prices are low
Investing a fixed amount on a schedule
Selling everything when the market drops
questions.explanation: Investing a set amount on a regular schedule can help smooth out the impact of market ups and downs over time.
